author: @Tom89194
text:
There is such a thing as a gravity battery that turns gravitational potential energy to electrical energy, however while lithium batteries can have 150 wh per kg on the low end, you would need to lift and lower more than 600 kg of mass 10 meters in height (using a motor/generator on the pully/gears) to get the same energy storage as a one kg low end lithium battery.
The stuff  I read suggests that these chemical gravity batteries were also desirable due to a low internal resistance since the solution densities keep the electrolyte separate without the need for a porous barrier.

I’m presuming that the copper sulphate and zinc sulphate would not degrade as long as they were correctly stored?? Thank you.

author: @MACLADILLY
text: I'm surprised😮. I thought a gravity battery was a mechanical battery system that used available energy ie solar or hydro to push a heavy material against gravity to a height where it was locked off until required. For example, using daytime solar power connected through a gearbox to an electric motor to lift the weight. At nighttime or whenever power is needed, the weight is slowly dropped which spins the the motor (DC) and generates electricity. So I was very surprised to see you demonstrating a chemical battery.

author: @mechanicalliteracyproject2508
text: I began collecting various items along the railroad tracks out here in central California. One interesting item I've found are Edison battery oil bottles. They have Thomas Edison's signature embossed on one side.  The railroads had large banks of batteries stored in concrete vaults burried in the ground  usually near a signal crossing. I've always wondered what battery oil was. As always, no matter the topic, you provide informative and entertaining videos. I think this could become a good science fair project.

author: @thesentientneuron6550
text: Hey Rob! Your videos on Zinc Bromide batteries were really intriguing, and it got me thinking about iodine (as in a zinc-iodide battery) but I found something even cooler. There’s a paper on Nature titled “A four-electron Zn-I2 aqueous battery enabled by reversible I−/I2/I+ conversion” by Y. Zou et. al. It discusses the formation and stabilisation of the ICl interhalogen allowing for an additional electron transfer reaction by the I+/I2 couple on top of the existing I2/I- couple (the latter mirroring the Br2/Br- couple of the Zinc Bromide battery). Please take a look and see if it lights a bulb over your head.

author: @justinklenk
text:
A true gravity battery, wherein a weight is raised, to provide later energy by its descent powering electricity generation, is the most ideally efficient battery we have - the only losses are any infinitesimal friction on the way up/down, and the loss of energy in the raising mechanism's energy transfer/powertrain and the loss of energy in the conversion of the descent to electricity to use, that last one obviously being the biggest, and in fact the ONLY truly significant loss in the whole system. 

That's why they are the (theoretical and actual) best possible battery that can exist, PERMANENTLY retaining 100% of its originally-stored energy (so it's not subject to electrical batteries' depotentiation over time in storage, nor even long-term chemical breakdown like gas or reagents, nor container seepage like hydrogen, nor pressure loss over time like air pressure, nor loss of a compressed material's springiness/rebound potential - it's just PERMANENT, PURE potential kinetic energy, eternally waiting for its triggered descent and simultaneous conversion to kinetic energy)... And, end-to-end, something like a 99.something-percent systemic storage efficiency, in practice.👍

It's god damn perfect!!

author: @Ammoniummetavanadate
text:
Closest we have to this today is a zinc-air battery, primary or secondary.

In the lab we had guys working on this for ultra low cost car batteries.

Problem with secondary zinc being dendrites causing shorting, it is hard to suppress.

author: @vidard9863
text: Wait, if i understand correctly the second battery, you could calculate the surface area needed to build a 12 or 24 volt cell?

replies.author: @NdxtremePro
replies.text: That is what I understood.

replies.author: @SasquatchTrevor
replies.text: No.  The voltage potential between zinc and bromine will always be 1.75 - 1.8.  I think he meant capacity, as in, how long the battery will run for after charging.  More surface area = longer discharge time.

replies.author: @vidard9863
replies.text:
@SasquatchTrevor no, the volume of the liquid and the density of it would determine the capacity, how long the battery can run, but the size of the... I guess anode and cathode is the correct term, could determine either amperage or possibly voltage.
What I don't understand is the need to up the voltage to charge it based on the size of the "cathode/anode". Probably the wrong term, but I think that you can guess what I mean.

replies.author: @SasquatchTrevor
replies.text: @vidard9863  Right.  That's what I meant.  With zinc/bromine, the more electrolyte you have, the greater the capacity.  I don't know about how it charges, though.

replies.author: @SasquatchTrevor
replies.text:
@vidard9863   Wait, I remember now.  So, with this type of battery, the electrolyte is the active material.  All that the electrodes act as is a current collector.  As the battery charges up, the zinc plates out on the anode, and bromine collects at the cathode.  The concentration of zinc bromide will go down, and the conductivity will drop.  I'd imagine that would have an effect on the voltage and current used to charge it up.

From what I have seen of this type of battery, is that often something like sodium bromide is reacted with zinc sulfate to form the zinc bromide.  The sodium sulfate that is left over is left in as a freerider electrolyte.  That has the benefit of maintaining conductivity.

author: @williamarmstrong7199
text:
Just an idea that might work or be fun to try. .
If you make a long tight coil of copper wire and drop a strong neodinium magnet down it it will produce power which can be stored in a capacitor and then fed to a motor driving a worm drive raising more magnets to the top, where they run down a slope before dropping into a funnel at the top of the coil to drop the next one down and produce yet more power etc etc.. Obviously the worm lift would need to be made from plastic or wood and the tube the magnets ride up of simalar none magnetic material.
Just think it might make a fun perpetual motion device? Or if not actualy perpetual then a small winding handle or a switchable battery to raise a number of magnets to the top ot the coil, to "prime" the system.

author: @rodh2168
text: Correct me if I'm wrong but the first "battery" was a generator that generated current from a chemical reaction that could be renewed with the addition of reactants while the second "battery" was in fact a rechargeable battery that stored power from a chemical reaction but the stored current needed to be renewed from an external source.  The first should be much more useful for longer-term applications.

replies.author: @MostlyPennyCat
replies.text: Correct, the first is a _primary_ cell, the second a _secondary_

replies.author: @pauliuspetronaitis1763
replies.text: @MostlyPennyCatIs it possible to manufacture a battery depicted in the first case, of a size that would allow producing, for example, 48 volts of energy and use it to power a 3-kilowatt inverter, for instance? And what quantities of materials would need to be considered in such a case?

replies.author: @MostlyPennyCat
replies.text:
@pauliuspetronaitis1763 
Well, the primary cell has an output of 1 Volt.
So you'd need 48 cells (a _battery)_ to create 48V.
Also we don't know the internal resistance of a cell.
Every cell in existence can be written as two components, a cell (or battery) of so many Volts and _zero_ resistance and an in-series resistor of some Ohms.
To calculate for a battery of cells, you just add up the voltages and the ohms.
This gives you the maximum current the battery can provide.
From that, you can calculate how much power and current you can generate.
Using:
V = I*R
P = V*I
I = V/R
Therefore P = V²/R

To _reduce_ the internal resistance of a battery, you wire batteries in parallel because then the formula for internal resistance becomes:
1/Rt = 1/R¹ + ... + 1/Rn
So you invert (divide 1 by R) all the individual radiators and sum them, then invert that number to get the total resistance.
Example:
Two 100 Ohm resistors wired in parallel.
1/100 + 1/100 = 1/50
1/(1/50) = 50.
50 Ohms.
Two resistors (or batteries) of 100 Ohms wired in parallel have a total resistance of 50 Ohms, half the separate batteries.

replies.author: @MostlyPennyCat
replies.text:
@pauliuspetronaitis1763 
Turns out this is a improvement on something called a "Daniell cell", you can look it up on Wikipedia.
From the section Gravity Cell:
_"Sometime during the 1860s, a Frenchman by the name of Callaud invented a variant of the Daniell cell which dispensed with the porous barrier. Instead, a layer of zinc sulfate sits on top of a layer of copper sulfate, the two liquids are kept separate by their differing densities, often with a layer of oil added on top to prevent evaporation. _*_This reduces the internal resistance of the system and thus the battery yields a stronger current."_*

Unfortunately it doesn't tell us what the internal resistance is, just that it's improved.
